message: bd-ys5f7 freeze FUSE kill-suidgid write flag parity

 {"id":"bd-ynv2","title":"Enable default-run FUSE E2E smoke tests with runtime skip gating","description":"Unignore a minimal stable subset of ffs-harness FUSE E2E tests that already self-skip when prerequisites are missing, so capable hosts get coverage by default without failing restricted environments.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-25T23:46:38.317832135Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-26T00:38:42.937747106Z","closed_at":"2026-02-26T00:38:42.937727900Z","close_reason":"Unignored fuse_getattr_file_metadata and fuse_readlink_and_symlink_detection — both are read-only smoke tests that already runtime-skip via fuse_available(). Now 5 tests run by default (read_hello_txt, readdir_root, read_nested_file, getattr_file_metadata, readlink_and_symlink_detection). Write-path and btrfs tests remain gated with #[ignore]. All gates pass (check, clippy, fmt).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-yrii","title":"JBD2 descriptor/revoke block checksum omits 4-byte tail instead of zeroing it","description":"In crates/ffs-journal/src/lib.rs, verify_jbd2_block_checksum handles JBD2_BLOCKTYPE_DESCRIPTOR and JBD2_BLOCKTYPE_REVOKE by computing crc32c over &block[..bs - 4]. However, Linux jbd2_descriptor_block_csum_verify temporarily zeroes the 4-byte tail checksum field and then computes the checksum over the *entire* block size (j->j_blocksize). By slicing off the last 4 bytes instead of hashing 4 zero bytes, the rust implementation produces a different CRC32C value and will incorrectly fail validation for valid JBD2 descriptor blocks.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-18T19:34:41.195653951Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-18T20:06:51.920432808Z","closed_at":"2026-04-18T20:06:51.918348189Z","close_reason":"Descriptor/revoke checksum verification now hashes the full block with a zeroed 4-byte tail, matching Linux jbd2 semantics; added regression coverage and passed targeted rch fmt/test/check/clippy.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"bd-ys5f7","title":"CONFORMANCE: freeze FUSE_WRITE_KILL_SUIDGID/RWF_SYNC collision","description":"Queue-empty porting-to-rust fallback. The vendored ABI exposes FUSE_WRITE_KILL_SUIDGID on write_flags bit 2, which numerically collides with Linux RWF_SYNC. FrankenFS intentionally derives synchronous write intent from the FUSE flags field, not raw write_flags, but current coverage only freezes the FUSE_WRITE_LOCKOWNER/RWF_DSYNC and FUSE_WRITE_CACHE/RWF_HIPRI collisions. Add focused dispatch coverage and parity evidence that FUSE_WRITE_KILL_SUIDGID does not trigger an accidental full fsync or other RWF side effect while preserving the normal write contract.","notes":"Claimed by Codex for porting-to-rust queue-empty parity fallback. Agent Mail health is red due missing schema tables, so coordination is recorded in Beads only.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-26T00:28:12.236659119Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-26T00:36:04.076070513Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T00:36:04.074160928Z","close_reason":"Completed: added FUSE_WRITE_KILL_SUIDGID/RWF_SYNC collision coverage, updated FEATURE_PARITY.md evidence, and validated targeted ffs-fuse test, parity report, ffs-fuse check/clippy, fmt, diff check, and UBS.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-yszx","title":"Add ext4 dir block checksum kernel-reference coverage","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-15T04:31:10.328110724Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-15T04:42:16.743572819Z","closed_at":"2026-04-15T04:42:16.740885570Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-yt66z","title":"[PERF] btrfs_ino_lookup scans entire fs-tree on every call — O(N) per ioctl with no caching","description":"# Performance concern from modes-of-reasoning pass

## Finding

`OpenFs::btrfs_resolve_inode_path` at crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:14447+ (added in f70dcca for bd-caex2) calls `self.walk_btrfs_fs_tree(cx)?` unconditionally on every invocation, walking every leaf entry in the subvolume fs-tree to build the `parent_by_child: BTreeMap` index. That means each BTRFS_IOC_INO_LOOKUP ioctl costs O(N) where N = total fs-tree items (inodes + dir entries + refs + xattrs + extents).

For a typical ext4/btrfs image the cost is negligible. For a large image (millions of inodes / tens of millions of items), a single ioctl can scan multi-megabyte indexes and iterate hundreds of thousands of entries. A caller issuing K ioctls imposes O(K*N) work — a slow query amplifier if a tool (e.g., `btrfs subvolume list`) iterates many inodes.

## Evidence

- Entry point: crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:14439 `fn btrfs_resolve_inode_path`
- Full-tree scan: `let entries = self.walk_btrfs_fs_tree(cx)?`
- Index built per call: BTreeMap<u64, (u64, Vec<u8>)> with one entry per INODE_REF item
- No cache, no early-termination (we build the full index before walking the chain even though we only need back-refs along the path)

## Fix directions (choose at implementation time)

1. **Early-exit walk.** Replace `walk_btrfs_fs_tree → build full index → walk` with a demand-driven search: at each step, search the fs-tree for items with key (objectid, INODE_REF, *) and stop at the first match. Each hop is an O(log N) btree lookup; total cost O(depth * log N).
2. **Per-subvolume cache** on `OpenFs` keyed on the fs-tree root generation. Invalidated on subvolume changes. Sub-μs per lookup after warmup.
3. **Batched multi-inode resolution.** If the caller is resolving multiple inodes in one request (e.g., a future `list_paths`), build the index once and amortize.

For V1, (1) is the simplest and keeps the code path stateless. (2) is the right long-term answer once we know the access pattern.

## Acceptance

- A synthetic image with 10K inodes completes a sequence of 100 INO_LOOKUP ioctls in bounded time (wall-clock cap, e.g., < 1s).
- Microbenchmark in `benches/` documents the per-ioctl cost.
- Unit tests from bd-caex2 continue to pass without regression.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-23T18:28:07.060519152Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-23T22:29:00.680913461Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T22:29:00.678366280Z","close_reason":"writable demand-driven walk + read-only cached index","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-yuhsu","title":"[FUSE] FIBMAP ioctl — legacy logical->physical block lookup (filefrag -B compat)","description":"FIBMAP (_IO(0x00, 1) = 0x00000001) is the legacy ioctl that returns the physical block number for a logical file block. Userspace passes a u32 input holding the logical block index; the kernel rewrites the same buffer with the physical block number on return. Used by filefrag -B, ext4 fsck, and e2image. ext4 implements via ext4_get_block which traverses the extent tree. Pre-fix dispatch_ioctl returns ENOTTY for FIBMAP through the FUSE mount. FrankenFS already has ffs_extent::map_logical_to_physical so the wiring is: parse u32 logical, route through OpenFs::physical_block_of(ino, logical), encode u32 physical (0 for hole). Acceptance: FIBMAP on a live file returns the same physical block reported by FIEMAP for the corresponding logical block; returns 0 for a hole; returns the existing physical block for an unwritten extent (matches kernel ext4 behaviour); rejects in_data smaller than 4 bytes with EINVAL.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-25T20:24:31.135369825Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-25T20:30:41.930158376Z","closed_at":"2026-04-25T20:30:41.928230106Z","close_reason":"FIBMAP shipped via dispatch_ioctl branch that routes through ops.fiemap with a 1-block window — leverages the existing extent-tree walker without adding a new FsOps method. 4 regressions cover EINVAL on short in/out, FIEMAP-equivalence for written data, and 0-physical for holes.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

@@ -286,7 +286,7 @@ behavior of that operation, not whether the row is unimplemented.
 | FUSE readlink | FrankenFS spec §9 | ✅ | `FsOps::readlink` via `OpenFs` |
 | FUSE mount runtime | FrankenFS spec §9 | ✅ | Production runtime lifecycle, signal handling, dispatch coverage, and CI-safe skip behavior are implemented; OQ4 (`bd-h6nz.6.4`) is resolved for V1.x with explicit `flush` (non-durable) vs `fsync`/`fsyncdir` (durability boundary) contract and writeback-cache-disabled policy. `crates/ffs-harness/tests/fuse_e2e.rs` now emits `SCENARIO_RESULT` coverage for ext4 (`ext4_rw_flush`, `ext4_rw_fdatasync`, `ext4_rw_fsync`, `ext4_rw_fsyncdir`, `ext4_rw_seek_data_hole`, `ext4_rw_seek_hole_fully_allocated`, `ext4_rw_seek_leading_hole`, `ext4_rw_seek_all_hole`) and btrfs (`btrfs_rw_flush`, `btrfs_rw_fdatasync`, `btrfs_rw_fsync`, `btrfs_rw_fsyncdir`, `btrfs_rw_seek_data_hole`, `btrfs_rw_seek_hole_fully_allocated`, `btrfs_rw_seek_leading_hole`, `btrfs_rw_seek_all_hole`) through real file flushes, file- and directory-FD sync calls, and mounted-path `SEEK_DATA`/`SEEK_HOLE` probes for punched-hole, fully allocated, leading-hole, and all-hole layouts with EOF `ENXIO` verification; btrfs read-only mounted-path `fsync`, `fdatasync`, and `fsyncdir` now also freeze exact `EROFS` with no data or dirent drift. |
 | FUSE ABI 7.40 protocol surface | FUSE kernel ABI 7.40 / libfuse 3.x | ✅ | Workspace builds the vendored `fuser` crate with `abi-7-40`, and `FrankenFuse::init` now negotiates `FUSE_SPLICE_READ`, `FUSE_SPLICE_WRITE`, `FUSE_SPLICE_MOVE`, and `FUSE_PASSTHROUGH` with stack depth 1 when the kernel advertises support. The vendored `fuser` protocol layer now exposes opcode 52 (`FUSE_STATX`), parses `fuse_statx_in`, replies with Linux statx-compatible `fuse_statx_out`, and preserves `FileAttr::crtime` as `STATX_BTIME`; `FrankenFuse::statx` routes that opcode through the existing `getattr` backend. The vendored ABI surface also exposes `FUSE_WRITE_KILL_SUIDGID`/legacy `FUSE_WRITE_KILL_PRIV` plus Linux `RWF_*` constants for write-intent propagation. Unit coverage freezes ABI constants, STATX request parsing, birth-time reply encoding, and mounted splice pipe-to-file transfer. |
-| FUSE pwritev2/io_uring RWF write intent propagation | `pwritev2(2)`, FUSE `WRITE` request flags | ✅ | `FrankenFuse::write` now decodes synchronous write intent from the FUSE `flags` field (`O_DSYNC`/`O_SYNC`) and routes it through `FsOps::fsync` after the normal MVCC commit, so `RWF_DSYNC` uses a data-sync boundary and `RWF_SYNC` uses a full sync boundary. The implementation deliberately does not treat `fuse_write_in.write_flags` bit 1 as `RWF_DSYNC`, because that bit is already `FUSE_WRITE_LOCKOWNER`; unit coverage freezes that collision and verifies no accidental fsync. `RWF_HIPRI` is treated as a harmless priority/cache hint because bit 0 is also `FUSE_WRITE_CACHE`; coverage freezes that it preserves the normal write contract without sync, NOWAIT, append, or unsupported-intent side effects. `RWF_NOWAIT` uses the non-conflicting RWF bit to attempt inode mutation-lock acquisition without blocking and returns `EAGAIN` when the lock is already held. `RWF_APPEND` and `O_APPEND` resolve the write offset to current EOF under the inode mutation guard, while `RWF_NOAPPEND` preserves the caller offset on an append-open file. Unsupported non-conflicting write intents that require guarantees FrankenFS does not provide yet (`RWF_ATOMIC` torn-write protection and `RWF_DONTCACHE` cache-bypass/drop semantics) reject with `EOPNOTSUPP` before backend mutation. `crates/ffs-fuse/src/lib.rs` unit tests cover DSync, Sync, flag-collision hints, NOWAIT fast-fail, append, NOAPPEND, and unsupported-intent rejection behavior, while `crates/ffs-harness/tests/fuse_e2e.rs` adds mounted-path `os.pwritev(...)` probes for both `RWF_DSYNC` and `RWF_SYNC`, proving requested bytes become immediately readable and survive remount when the host permits FUSE. |
+| FUSE pwritev2/io_uring RWF write intent propagation | `pwritev2(2)`, FUSE `WRITE` request flags | ✅ | `FrankenFuse::write` now decodes synchronous write intent from the FUSE `flags` field (`O_DSYNC`/`O_SYNC`) and routes it through `FsOps::fsync` after the normal MVCC commit, so `RWF_DSYNC` uses a data-sync boundary and `RWF_SYNC` uses a full sync boundary. The implementation deliberately does not treat `fuse_write_in.write_flags` bit 1 as `RWF_DSYNC`, because that bit is already `FUSE_WRITE_LOCKOWNER`; unit coverage freezes that collision and verifies no accidental fsync. `RWF_HIPRI` is treated as a harmless priority/cache hint because bit 0 is also `FUSE_WRITE_CACHE`; coverage freezes that it preserves the normal write contract without sync, NOWAIT, append, or unsupported-intent side effects. `FUSE_WRITE_KILL_SUIDGID` is likewise kept as a metadata write hint despite sharing raw bit 2 with `RWF_SYNC`; unit coverage freezes that it preserves the normal write contract without an accidental full fsync. `RWF_NOWAIT` uses the non-conflicting RWF bit to attempt inode mutation-lock acquisition without blocking and returns `EAGAIN` when the lock is already held. `RWF_APPEND` and `O_APPEND` resolve the write offset to current EOF under the inode mutation guard, while `RWF_NOAPPEND` preserves the caller offset on an append-open file. Unsupported non-conflicting write intents that require guarantees FrankenFS does not provide yet (`RWF_ATOMIC` torn-write protection and `RWF_DONTCACHE` cache-bypass/drop semantics) reject with `EOPNOTSUPP` before backend mutation. `crates/ffs-fuse/src/lib.rs` unit tests cover DSync, Sync, flag-collision hints, NOWAIT fast-fail, append, NOAPPEND, and unsupported-intent rejection behavior, while `crates/ffs-harness/tests/fuse_e2e.rs` adds mounted-path `os.pwritev(...)` probes for both `RWF_DSYNC` and `RWF_SYNC`, proving requested bytes become immediately readable and survive remount when the host permits FUSE. |
 | FUSE inotify rename + dentry notification lifecycle | `fs/notify/fsnotify.c`, `fs/notify/inotify/inotify_user.c`, FUSE notify protocol | ✅ | `FrankenFuse` captures a `fuser::Notifier` during blocking, background, and managed mount setup, then issues `FUSE_NOTIFY_INVAL_ENTRY` for successfully committed `unlink`, `rmdir`, and both old/new directory entries after `rename`. Plain renames deliberately avoid `FUSE_NOTIFY_DELETE`, preserving the kernel/VFS `IN_MOVED_FROM` + `IN_MOVED_TO` cookie-pair contract instead of synthesizing delete/create observations. `crates/ffs-harness/tests/fuse_e2e.rs` now carries mounted-path inotify probes for 100 same-parent renames and a cross-directory rename; both assert non-zero matching cookies and reject `IN_CREATE`, `IN_DELETE`, `IN_Q_OVERFLOW`, and `IN_IGNORED` during plain rename bursts, with the standard CI soft-skip when the current host denies FUSE mounting. |
 | FUSE ioctl FIEMAP | `include/linux/fiemap.h` | ✅ | `FsOps::fiemap` queries ext4 extent tree via `collect_extents_with_scope` and btrfs extent data items via `btrfs_fiemap_extent_items`. FUSE `ioctl` handler parses `FS_IOC_FIEMAP` (0xC020660B), rejects unsupported request flags with `EBADR`, honors `FIEMAP_FLAG_SYNC` by fsyncing writable mounts before extent lookup, and marshals the fiemap header plus extent array. `crates/ffs-core` now has deterministic btrfs coverage for inline extents (`physical=0`, `FIEMAP_EXTENT_LAST`) and keep-size prealloc extents (`FIEMAP_EXTENT_UNWRITTEN`), while `crates/ffs-harness/tests/fuse_e2e.rs` verifies mounted-path FIEMAP reporting for ext4 regular files, ext4 `SYNC` and invalid-request-flag contracts, plus btrfs regular, inline, and keep-size prealloc files. Note: FUSE transport may not deliver this ioctl to userspace on all kernel versions. |
 | FUSE ioctl EXT4_IOC_GETFLAGS | `fs/ext4/ioctl.c` | ✅ | `FsOps::get_inode_flags` returns raw ext4 `i_flags` field. FUSE `ioctl` handler dispatches `EXT4_IOC_GETFLAGS` (0x80086601). Harness integration coverage in `crates/ffs-harness/tests/fuse_e2e.rs` now verifies mounted-path `GETFLAGS` on a writable ext4 file. |
